text.title: GraphRAG Implementation Checklist and Cost Model
text.summary: Phased implementation checklist, quality validation thresholds, cost model, and agent-specific gotchas for GraphRAG deployments.

list.item: Phase 1 — Planning: define entity ontology, choose graph DB, select extraction method, set accuracy vs cost trade-offs
Phase 2 — Graph construction: entity extraction → entity resolution → relationship extraction → graph write
Phase 3 — Community detection: Leiden algorithm → hierarchical summarization → cache summaries
Phase 4 — Retrieval: query classifier → local/global router → context assembler
Phase 5 — Production: incremental indexing, query latency monitoring, community refresh strategy

rule.statement: After graph construction: entity extraction F1 must exceed 0.75; relationship accuracy must exceed 0.70; zero duplicate entities.
rule.rationale: Below these thresholds, community detection produces semantically incoherent clusters that degrade global search quality.

rule.statement: After community detection: communities must be semantically coherent; hierarchical levels must be meaningful; summaries must capture key themes.
rule.rationale: Incoherent communities cause global queries to return irrelevant summaries; the LLM synthesizes from noise.

rule.statement: Production readiness: P95 query latency under 3s, error rate under 1%, hallucination rate under 10%.
rule.rationale: GraphRAG is inherently slower than vector RAG; 3s is the practical ceiling for interactive use cases.

section.title: Cost Model
section.p: Indexing: entity extraction ~500 tokens/chunk, relationship extraction ~300 tokens/chunk, community summarization ~1000 tokens/community. Query: global ~2000 tokens, local ~500 tokens.

rule.statement: Batch entity extraction chunks in groups of 10-20 per LLM call to reduce API overhead by 5-8x.
rule.rationale: Single-chunk extraction calls are the primary cost driver. Batching keeps extraction cost proportional to corpus size rather than chunk count.

rule.statement: Use LightRAG for cost-sensitive workloads where 10x token reduction is worth the trade-off in relationship granularity.
rule.rationale: LightRAG uses dual-level graph retrieval with significantly fewer LLM calls. Not suitable for legal/medical precision requirements.

antipattern.description: Deploying GraphRAG without a human review checkpoint for graph writes from unverified sources.
antipattern.reason: One hallucinated relationship from entity extraction can corrupt multi-hop reasoning for all subsequent queries across the affected entities.

antipattern.description: Relying on Leiden community detection to be deterministic across rebuilds.
antipattern.reason: Leiden is non-deterministic; rebuilding the index with identical data produces different community structures. Do not use community IDs as stable references.

antipattern.description: Using LightRAG for legal or medical knowledge graphs requiring precise relationship granularity.
antipattern.reason: LightRAG's 10x token reduction compresses relationship metadata. Fine-grained distinctions between relationship types are lost.
